narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 48% chance that you might not pass the bar exam on your first try, which is the threshold we use to make these tough admission decisions. Your profile shows solid strengths, like an undergraduate GPA of 3.40 that's above the typical 3.21 average, and coming from a middle-income family while planning to study full-time, which is the norm for most applicants. However, some areas stood out as concerns: your Year 1 school decile of 3 is below the average of about 5.7, your Year 3 decile of 2 is well under the typical 5.5, and your LSAT score of 31 falls short of the usual 36.7. As a female applicant with a Black background, you're part of smaller groups in the dataset—women make up 44% and Black students just 5%—but these demographics played smaller roles. The model's decision was driven most strongly by your Year 3 decile, which significantly nudged the prediction toward expecting a bar exam struggle, likely because lower performance there signals potential challenges ahead. Next in influence was your LSAT score, which also pushed in the same direction by being below par for students who typically pass. Your Year 1 decile ranked third, reinforcing that early academic hurdles mattered a lot here. Family income came fourth, followed by racial background, undergraduate GPA, gender, and full-time status, all contributing somewhat toward the failure prediction but with less weight. In comparison to typical students who pass, your lower deciles and LSAT put you at a disadvantage, even with your stronger GPA pulling slightly the other way. While we can't change the past, you could realistically boost your future bar exam odds by retaking the LSAT to aim higher—many students improve with targeted prep—and focusing on building stronger study habits to overcome those decile weaknesses, perhaps through tutoring or exam strategies. Law schools value persistence, so consider reapplying after showing progress in these areas. Hang in there; with effort on test prep and academics, your path to success is still very much open.
